images.caption: Figure 1:Distribution of question types in existing medical datasets. Only 22% are reasoning-intensive, and just 3% among them concern rare diseases.

Figure 2:Left: Comparison of existing methods for medical reasoning.
“SS RL” is short for self-supervised RL.
“Avg Token/Sample” denotes the average number of tokens consumed from an API model to generate one sample.Right: Performance improvement comparison based on Llama.
Prior methods show less improvement in rare diseases than common tasks, while ours significantly breaks the rare disease improvement upper bound of+3%+3\%.

Figure 3:Overview of the proposed method.
We first synthesize questions with tunable distribution, then train the policy model via a semi-supervised reinforcement learning mechanism.
Rare disease knowledge injection is triggered when the random valueρ\rhois less than the thresholdα\alpha.
Figure 4:Average performance on rare disease and general datasets across different rare disease ratios.

Figure 5:Impact of rare disease knowledge injection.
The upper/lower part denotes rare/general tasks.
Figure 6:Rewards (left) and performance (right) curves of different labeling strategies across training steps.
Figure 7:Comparison of our question-only synthesis and long reasoning chain synthesis (SFT).

Figure 8:Rewards and performance curves of offline labeling with more than 1K steps.
We report the average performance on test sets with error bars.
